narrative:
Pinebrook Advisory Group is a five-person management consultancy serving
mid-market operating companies. The firm takes on a handful of engagements
a year: operational reviews, pricing studies, and post-acquisition
integration support. Its documents are lean and practical: engagement
letters on firm letterhead, kickoff memos, meeting minutes that name every
attendee, and status reports written for a client executive who skims.
The Managing Partner signs everything that leaves the building. Tone is
plain American business English, first names inside the firm, full names
and titles when a client is on the page.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0003",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- If a doc carries `engagement_position`, honor it: it states where the
  document sits in its engagement's timeline. Do not describe progress or a
  phase inconsistent with it, and do not state the engagement's start or
  end date (those are fact placeholders, not yours to write).
- If a doc carries `firm_digest`, it is the ONLY firm history you may treat
  as established as of this document's date. Reference clients only through
  the placeholders it names; claim no engagement it does not list; present
  nothing later than this document as already true.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Integration Support for Reeves, Miller and Thompson

authors.name: Daniel Jones
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Daniel is the firm's final signature and knows it; nothing reaches a client until he has read it end to end and can stand behind every line. He thinks in terms of what the client's own executive will take away from a single skim, and he trims anything that does not survive that test. His voice is plain, confident, and unhurried, the senior partner who would rather say one useful thing clearly than three that hedge.

participants.name: Cynthia Ball
participants.title: Analyst
participants.persona: Cynthia is the analyst who likes a hard question and a spreadsheet to answer it, most in her element pulling apart a pricing question until the drivers are clear. She is sharp and a little skeptical, quick to ask whether an assumption actually holds. Her drafts are crisp and evidence-first, laid out so a principal can see how she got there and lift what they need.

participants.name: Robert Miller
participants.title: Principal Consultant
participants.persona: Robert carries engagements from kickoff through delivery and treats the client relationship as his to protect. He is most at home in an operational review, walking a floor and turning what he sees into a short list of things worth fixing. His writing is direct and practical, built around recommendations a busy operator can act on, and he would rather be concrete than impressive.

participants.name: Jennifer Fletcher
participants.title: Senior Consultant
participants.persona: Jennifer leads engagements with a structured, methodical hand and is the partner most likely to insist the numbers reconcile before anyone briefs the client. She gravitates toward pricing studies and the discipline of integration work, where sequence and follow-through matter. Her tone is measured and organized; she runs a tight meeting and writes minutes that leave no attendee guessing what they agreed to.

docs.engagement_summary: Integration Support for Reeves, Miller and Thompson, running about 7 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

facts.hint: amount of money; place where the figure belongs
calendar date; place where the date belongs
proper name; place where the name belongs

docs.guidance: Formal letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement: scope, approach, team, fee and start date, closing. End with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact. 3-6 paragraphs.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 0% of the way through the engagement (at or before kickoff); it runs roughly 7 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.
